meta.title: TGD Actor Network Graph
meta.description: Interactive network of relationships between militant actors and organisations profiled by The Global Decipher.

nodes.label: Osama bin Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden
nodes.role: Founder and Emir of Al-Qaeda
nodes.summary: Born to the wealthy Saudi bin Laden construction family, Osama bin Laden travelled to Pakistan after the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan in 1979 to support the mujahideen, working with Palestinian Islamist Abdullah Azzam through Maktab al-Khidamat. In 1988 he founded al-Qaeda as a vanguard for transnational jihad.

nodes.label: Ayman Mohammed Rabie al-Zawahiri
nodes.role: Second Emir of Al-Qaeda; chief ideologue and strategist
nodes.summary: Trained as a surgeon in Cairo and from a distinguished Egyptian family, al-Zawahiri joined the Muslim Brotherhood as a teenager and was imprisoned and tortured after the 1981 assassination of Anwar Sadat. He led Egyptian Islamic Jihad through the 1990s, directing the 1995 bombing of the Egyptian embassy in Islamabad and the 1997 Luxor massacre.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $25 million

nodes.label: Mohammed Salahaldin Abd El Halim Zidane
nodes.role: De facto leader of Al-Qaeda (since 2022); long-time head of al-Qaeda's Military Committee
nodes.summary: A former lieutenant-colonel in Egypt's special forces, Saif al-Adel was implicated in the 1981 assassination of President Anwar Sadat and later joined Egyptian Islamic Jihad. He travelled to Afghanistan in the late 1980s, trained mujahideen, and became one of al-Qaeda's earliest military commanders.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $10 million

nodes.label: Hamza bin Osama bin Mohammed bin Awad bin Laden
nodes.role: Heir-apparent figurehead of al-Qaeda; messaging lead targeting global audiences
nodes.summary: Hamza bin Laden was a favoured son of Osama bin Laden who grew up in Afghanistan and was being groomed as a future al-Qaeda leader. After 2001 he and his family lived in Iran under IRGC custody, then made his way to the Afghanistan/Pakistan border region.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $1 million

nodes.label: Ahmad Fadeel al-Nazal al-Khalayleh
nodes.role: Founder of Al-Qaeda in Iraq, the organisational forerunner of ISIS
nodes.summary: A Jordanian street criminal turned jihadist after a prison stint with the cleric Abu Muhammad al-Maqdisi, Zarqawi ran a training camp in Herat, Afghanistan before relocating to Iraqi Kurdistan via Iran in 2002. He came to global attention through Colin Powell's February 2003 UN speech that wrongly tied him to Saddam Hussein's WMD program. After the 2003 U.S.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $25 million at time of death

nodes.label: Ibrahim Awad Ibrahim al-Badri al-Samarrai
nodes.role: Self-proclaimed Caliph of the Islamic State (2014–2019)
nodes.summary: A doctoral graduate in Quranic studies from the Islamic University of Baghdad, Baghdadi was detained at U.S. Camp Bucca in 2004, where he expanded his jihadist network. He rose through the Islamic State of Iraq, becoming its leader in 2010 after the deaths of Abu Omar al-Baghdadi and Abu Ayyub al-Masri.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $25 million

nodes.label: Amir Muhammad Sa'id Abdal-Rahman al-Mawla
nodes.role: Second Caliph of the Islamic State
nodes.summary: A Turkmen Iraqi with a master's degree in Quranic studies, al-Mawla was a religious scholar in Saddam-era Iraq and reportedly served as a low-level officer or Islamic Studies teacher before joining al-Qaeda in Iraq. Captured by U.S. forces in 2008, he became a prolific Camp Bucca informant before being released.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $10 million

nodes.label: Unknown (Iraqi national)
nodes.role: Third Caliph of the Islamic State
nodes.summary: A relatively obscure ISIS figure whose true identity was never publicly confirmed by ISIS, Abu al-Hassan al-Hashimi al-Qurashi was named Caliph in March 2022 in an audio statement by spokesman Abu Omar al-Muhajir following the death of al-Mawla. His tenure was brief and his public footprint negligible.

nodes.label: Unknown
nodes.role: Fourth Caliph of the Islamic State
nodes.summary: Named Caliph by ISIS in November 2022, Abu al-Hussein al-Husseini al-Qurashi's identity was never confirmed publicly. Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan announced on 30 April 2023 that Turkish national intelligence (MIT) had killed him in a 29 April operation in northern Syria. ISIS confirmed his death and announced a successor in August 2023.

nodes.label: Unknown (assessed by UN to potentially be Abdallah Makki Mosleh al-Rafi'i / Abu Khadija al-Iraqi)
nodes.role: Current (5th) Caliph of the Islamic State
nodes.summary: Named Caliph by ISIS spokesman Abu Hudhayfa al-Ansari in an audio statement on 3 August 2023, Abu Hafs al-Hashimi al-Qurashi is the fifth Caliph since the death of Baghdadi.

nodes.label: Anwar Nasser al-Awlaki
nodes.role: AQAP propagandist; chief recruiter for English-speaking jihadists; external operations planner
nodes.summary: Born in the U.S. to Yemeni parents, al-Awlaki returned to Yemen as a child and then back to the U.S. for college, becoming an imam in San Diego and later at Dar al-Hijrah in Falls Church, Virginia, where he interacted with three of the future 9/11 hijackers.

nodes.label: Khalid Sheikh Mohammed
nodes.role: Principal architect of the September 11 attacks; chief of al-Qaeda's external operations until capture
nodes.summary: An engineering graduate of North Carolina A&T State University, KSM became the architect of al-Qaeda's most ambitious external operations. He helped fund his nephew Ramzi Yousef's 1993 World Trade Center bombing and co-planned the 1995 Bojinka plot to blow up 12 U.S. airliners over the Pacific.
designations: US Military Commissions — Defendant in U.S. v. Khalid Shaikh Mohammad et al.

nodes.label: Abdul Basit Mahmoud Abdul Karim
nodes.role: Principal bomb-maker of the 1993 World Trade Center bombing; co-architect of the Bojinka plot
nodes.summary: Nephew of KSM, Yousef trained in electrical engineering in Wales and studied bomb-making at al-Qaeda camps in Afghanistan in the early 1990s. He entered the U.S. on a false passport in September 1992 and led the 26 February 1993 truck bombing of the World Trade Center's North Tower garage, which killed 6 and injured over 1,000.

nodes.label: Nasir Abdul-Karim Abdullah al-Wuhayshi
nodes.role: Founding Emir of AQAP; second-in-command of al-Qaeda central under al-Zawahiri
nodes.summary: Served as Osama bin Laden's personal secretary in Afghanistan in the late 1990s, fleeing to Iran after 2001 and being deported to Yemen, where he was held until escaping from a Sanaa prison in 2006. He led the merger of Yemeni and Saudi al-Qaeda branches that produced AQAP in January 2009.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $10 million

nodes.label: Khalid Saeed Batarfi
nodes.role: Third Emir of AQAP
nodes.summary: A long-time AQAP figure who served as the group's senior Sharia official, Batarfi was held at Yemen's Mansoura Central Prison until a 2015 mass jailbreak. He served as AQAP's military chief and as Emir of Hadhramaut before being named Emir in February 2020, succeeding Qassim al-Rimi.

nodes.label: Mohammed Omar Mujahid
nodes.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Taliban; first head of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan (1996–2001)
nodes.summary: A poor village mullah and Soviet-war veteran who lost his right eye to shrapnel, Omar founded the Taliban in 1994 with former madrassa students in Kandahar, capitalising on the chaos and lawlessness of the post-Soviet civil war.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $10 million

nodes.label: Akhtar Mohammad Mansour
nodes.role: Second Supreme Leader of the Taliban; previously Taliban Minister of Civil Aviation (1996–2001)
nodes.summary: A senior Taliban figure under Mullah Omar, Mansour served as Minister of Civil Aviation in the 1996–2001 Islamic Emirate and was widely seen as the de facto operational head of the Taliban after Omar's death — which he concealed for two years.

nodes.label: Hibatullah Akhundzada
nodes.role: Third and current Supreme Leader of the Taliban (2016–present); Head of State of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan (2021–present)
nodes.summary: A religious scholar rather than military commander, Akhundzada served as the Taliban's chief justice during the 1996–2001 Islamic Emirate, issuing the regime's most consequential rulings on stoning, amputations, and the destruction of the Bamiyan Buddhas. He was selected as Emir in May 2016 within days of Mansour's death.

nodes.label: Sirajuddin Jalaluddin Haqqani
nodes.role: Acting Interior Minister of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan (since 2021); Deputy Supreme Leader of Taliban; head of Haqqani Network
nodes.summary: Eldest son of Mujahideen-era commander Jalaluddin Haqqani, Sirajuddin assumed operational control of the Haqqani Network in the late 2000s as his father's health declined.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $10 million (active)

nodes.label: Jalaluddin Haqqani
nodes.role: Founder of the Haqqani Network; legendary anti-Soviet mujahideen commander
nodes.summary: A Pashtun cleric educated at the Darul Uloom Haqqania madrasa in Akora Khattak (from which he took his nom de guerre), Haqqani became one of the most effective anti-Soviet mujahideen commanders of the 1980s, receiving significant CIA, ISI, and Saudi support.

nodes.label: Mohammad Yaqoob Mujahid
nodes.role: Acting Defence Minister of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan; head of Taliban Military Commission
nodes.summary: Eldest son of Mullah Omar, Yaqoob studied at the Akora Khattak madrasa in Pakistan and emerged into Taliban politics after his father's death in 2013. Promoted to head the Taliban Military Commission in 2020, he became Acting Defence Minister in September 2021.

nodes.label: Baitullah Mehsud
nodes.role: Founding Emir of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan
nodes.summary: A barely-literate Mehsud tribesman from South Waziristan, Baitullah Mehsud emerged from the post-2001 Pakistani Taliban consolidation as the dominant commander among Mehsud groups.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Hakimullah Mehsud
nodes.role: Second Emir of TTP
nodes.summary: Originally a driver and bodyguard to Baitullah Mehsud, Hakimullah rose to lead TTP operations in Orakzai, Kurram, and Khyber tribal areas before being elevated to Emir in August 2009.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Fazal Hayat
nodes.role: Third Emir of TTP
nodes.summary: Son-in-law of TNSM founder Sufi Mohammad, Fazlullah ran a pirate FM radio station in Swat from 2006 broadcasting calls for sharia and against girls' education. He led the Taliban takeover of Swat in 2007–2009, imposing brutal sharia rule. Driven out by the Pakistani army's Operation Rah-e-Rast in 2009, he relocated to Kunar and Nuristan provinces in Afghanistan.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Mufti Noor Wali Mehsud
nodes.role: Current Emir of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan
nodes.summary: An Islamic scholar (mufti) from the Mechikhel sub-clan of the Mehsud tribe, Noor Wali received madrasa education and fought alongside the Afghan Taliban in the 1990s before joining TTP. He served as TTP qadi (judge), Karachi commander, and deputy to Mullah Fazlullah.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $3 million

nodes.label: Maulana Masood Azhar Alvi
nodes.role: Founder and Emir of Jaish-e-Mohammed
nodes.summary: Educated at Karachi's Jamia Uloom-ul-Islamia (Banuri Town), Azhar joined Harkat-ul-Mujahideen and became its general secretary. He was arrested by Indian forces in Kashmir in 1994 and released in December 1999 along with Omar Saeed Sheikh in exchange for hostages of hijacked Indian Airlines Flight 814 in Kandahar.
designations: UN 1267 — Sanctions (added 2019 after long China veto) — (QDi.428)
India MHA — Listed terrorist under UAPA

nodes.label: Hafiz Muhammad Saeed
nodes.role: Founder of Lashkar-e-Taiba; Emir of Jamaat-ud-Dawa
nodes.summary: An Islamic studies academic at the University of Engineering and Technology Lahore, Saeed co-founded Markaz Dawa wal Irshad in 1985 with Zafar Iqbal and a Saudi religious scholar, Sheikh Abdullah Azzam, to spread Ahl-e-Hadith Salafism in Pakistan.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $10 million
India NIA — Listed accused in 26/11 Mumbai attacks

nodes.label: Zaki-ur-Rehman Lakhvi
nodes.role: LeT military chief; principal operational planner of the 2008 Mumbai attacks
nodes.summary: A founding LeT military commander, Lakhvi ran LeT's training camps in Muzaffarabad, Pakistan-administered Kashmir, and supervised the 2008 Mumbai attacks operation in real time from a Karachi safehouse — including phone instructions to the gunmen during the three-day siege. Arrested in Pakistan in December 2008 under heavy Indian and U.S.

nodes.label: Sajid Mir
nodes.role: LeT external operations chief; principal handler during 2008 Mumbai attacks
nodes.summary: Lashkar-e-Taiba's most consequential external operations officer, Mir personally directed the 2008 Mumbai attacks via phone from LeT's Karachi control room — voice recordings of his instructions to the gunmen, recorded by Indian intelligence, are central to the global case against him. He also recruited and ran U.S.
designations: UN 1267 — Sanctions (added 2022 after long Chinese hold)
US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Mohammad Ilyas Kashmiri
nodes.role: Operational commander of HuJI's 313 Brigade and al-Qaeda military operations chief in South Asia
nodes.summary: A former Pakistani Special Services Group commando turned jihadist, Kashmiri reportedly trained Pakistani-aligned Kashmiri militants in the 1990s and was once celebrated by then-Army Chief Pervez Musharraf for presenting an Indian soldier's severed head.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Bashir Zeb Baloch
nodes.org: Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) – Majeed Brigade direction
nodes.role: Reported chief of BLA; commander overseeing the Majeed Brigade suicide squad
designations: US State Dept — BLA designated FTO/SDGT, 2019
tags: Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) – Majeed Brigade direction

nodes.label: Allah Nazar Baloch
nodes.role: Chief commander of the Balochistan Liberation Front
nodes.summary: Allah Nazar is a former medical student at the Bolan Medical College who became the public face of the post-2003 'middle-class' phase of the Baloch insurgency, distinct from older tribal-based movements (Bugti, Marri, Mengal).

nodes.label: Ahmed Hussein al-Sharaa
nodes.role: President of the Syrian Arab Republic (interim); former Emir of Hayat Tahrir al-Sham
nodes.summary: From a Syrian family with roots in the Israeli-occupied Golan Heights, Jolani travelled to Iraq after the 2003 U.S. invasion and fought with al-Qaeda in Iraq. He was captured by U.S. forces and held at Camp Bucca (2006–2011). Released and sent by Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi to establish a Syrian front during the Arab Spring, he founded Jabhat al-Nusra in 2012.
designations: US State Dept — SDGT designation since 2013; status under review post-2024
tags: Formerly Salafi-jihadism; since 2016 Islamist Syrian nationalist
nodes.category: Formerly Salafi-jihadism; since 2016 Islamist Syrian nationalist

nodes.label: Abubakar Mohammed Shekau
nodes.org: Boko Haram (Jama'atu Ahlis Sunna Lidda'awati wal-Jihad)
nodes.role: Second leader of Boko Haram
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $7 million
tags: Boko Haram (Jama'atu Ahlis Sunna Lidda'awati wal-Jihad)

nodes.label: Habib Yusuf
nodes.role: Leader of ISWAP; son of Boko Haram founder Mohammed Yusuf
nodes.summary: Son of Boko Haram founder Mohammed Yusuf, Habib Yusuf was named leader of Islamic State's West Africa Province by ISIS in August 2016, displacing Abubakar Shekau on the grounds that Shekau's takfirism alienated local Muslims. The Shekau loyalists refused, splintering Boko Haram.

nodes.label: Abdelmalek Droukdel
nodes.role: Founding Emir of AQIM
nodes.summary: A trained electrical engineer who joined Algeria's Armed Islamic Group (GIA) in 1996, Droukdel became Emir of GSPC in 2004 and engineered its 2007 rebranding as Al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb following formal pledged bayah to bin Laden.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Iyad ag Ghaly
nodes.role: Emir of JNIM, al-Qaeda's principal Sahel affiliate
nodes.summary: A Tuareg of the influential Ifoghas tribe from Kidal, Ag Ghaly trained with Gaddafi's Pan-Africanist Islamic Corps in Libya (1978–90), fought against Israel in Lebanon in the early 1980s, and led successive Tuareg rebellions in Mali (1988 MPLA, 2006 Kidal accord). He gradually radicalised through the Tablighi Jamaat and contacts with AQIM.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million

nodes.label: Lehbib Ould Ali Ould Said Ould Joumani
nodes.org: Islamic State in the Greater Sahara (ISGS)
nodes.role: Emir of ISGS, the Islamic State's Sahel branch
nodes.summary: A Sahrawi who fought with Polisario in his youth, al-Sahrawi rose through MUJAO (Movement for Oneness and Jihad in West Africa), then al-Mourabitoun under Mokhtar Belmokhtar. He broke with Belmokhtar in May 2015 to pledge bayah to Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi, founding ISGS.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $5 million
tags: Islamic State in the Greater Sahara (ISGS)

nodes.label: Ahmed Abdi Mohammed
nodes.role: First Emir of Al-Shabaab
nodes.summary: Trained in Pakistani madrasas and Afghan al-Qaeda camps before 2001, Godane returned to Somalia and rose through the Islamic Courts Union. He became Emir of Al-Shabaab after its 2008 formal break from the ICU and pledged bayah to al-Qaeda in 2012, formalising AQ's East African affiliate.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $7 million

nodes.label: Ahmed Diriye
nodes.role: Emir of Al-Shabaab
nodes.summary: A senior Al-Shabaab sharia official and previously the head of its Amniyat intelligence wing, Diriye was elevated to Emir within days of Godane's killing in September 2014.
designations: US Rewards for Justice — Bounty up to $6 million

nodes.label: Encep Nurjaman
nodes.role: JI operations chief; al-Qaeda's principal Southeast Asia liaison
nodes.summary: Hambali fought against the Soviets in Afghanistan in the late 1980s and returned to Southeast Asia to help build Jemaah Islamiyah under Abdullah Sungkar and Abu Bakar Bashir. He was Operations Chief and head of JI's Malaysian-Singapore Mantiqi I region.

nodes.label: Abu Bakar Bashir
nodes.role: Co-founder and spiritual leader of Jemaah Islamiyah; founder of JAT
nodes.summary: An Indonesian-born cleric of Hadhrami Arab descent, Bashir co-founded Pondok Pesantren al-Mukmin (the Ngruki pesantren) with Abdullah Sungkar in 1972 and fled to Malaysia in 1985 to escape Suharto's New Order. He co-founded Jemaah Islamiyah in 1993, succeeding Sungkar as Emir in 1999.
designations: Indonesia BNPT — Listed; JI and JAT designated

nodes.label: Anders Behring Breivik
nodes.role: Perpetrator of the 22 July 2011 Norway attacks
nodes.summary: A Norwegian far-right gunman who detonated a fertiliser bomb outside the Norwegian government quarter in Oslo (killing 8) and then drove to the Workers' Youth League summer camp on Utøya island, where he shot dead 69 people, mostly teenagers, in a 72-minute massacre.
designations: Norway Penal Code — Convicted, 21-yr preventive detention

nodes.label: Brenton Harrison Tarrant
nodes.role: Perpetrator of the 15 March 2019 Christchurch mosque attacks
nodes.summary: An Australian white-supremacist who livestreamed his attacks on the Al Noor and Linwood mosques in Christchurch, killing 51 worshippers in Friday prayer. He published a 74-page manifesto, 'The Great Replacement,' modelled explicitly on Breivik's.
designations: New Zealand — Convicted, life without parole

nodes.label: Dylann Storm Roof
nodes.role: Perpetrator of the 17 June 2015 Charleston church shooting
nodes.summary: A self-radicalised white-supremacist, Roof entered a Wednesday evening Bible study at Mother Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal Church in Charleston, sat with worshippers for about an hour, then opened fire — killing nine African-American congregants including the church's pastor and state senator Clementa Pinckney.
designations: US Federal Court — Convicted federal hate crimes; capital sentence

nodes.label: Timothy James McVeigh
nodes.role: Perpetrator of the 19 April 1995 Oklahoma City bombing
nodes.summary: A decorated U.S. Army Gulf War veteran radicalised by the 1992 Ruby Ridge standoff, the 1993 Waco siege, and the militia/Patriot movement, McVeigh — with Terry Nichols — built a 4,800-pound ANFO truck bomb and detonated it outside the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in downtown Oklahoma City on the second anniversary of Waco.

nodes.label: Patrick Wood Crusius
nodes.role: Perpetrator of the 3 August 2019 El Paso Walmart shooting
nodes.summary: Crusius drove ~650 miles from suburban Dallas to El Paso and opened fire with an AK-style rifle inside a Cielo Vista Walmart, killing 23 people — mostly Hispanic and Mexican shoppers — and wounding 22.
designations: US Federal Court — Convicted, 90 consecutive life sentences

nodes.label: Payton S. Gendron
nodes.role: Perpetrator of the 14 May 2022 Buffalo Tops supermarket shooting
nodes.summary: Gendron drove ~200 miles from Conklin, NY to a predominantly Black neighbourhood in east Buffalo and opened fire with an AR-15-style rifle inside Tops Friendly Markets, killing 10 Black shoppers and wounding three others.

nodes.label: Velupillai Prabhakaran
nodes.role: Founder and Supreme Commander of the LTTE
nodes.summary: Prabhakaran founded the LTTE in May 1976 in response to Sinhala-majority discrimination against Sri Lanka's Tamil minority.
designations: Sri Lanka — Convicted in absentia (Rajiv Gandhi assassination linked)
India CBI — Listed, Rajiv Gandhi assassination case
US State Dept — LTTE FTO designated 1997

nodes.label: Joseph Rao Kony
nodes.role: Founder and leader of the Lord's Resistance Army
nodes.summary: A cousin of Holy Spirit Movement leader Alice Lakwena, Kony took over remnants of Acholi rebels in 1987 and built the LRA as a millenarian Christian insurgency against Uganda's Museveni government, claiming to want to rule Uganda by the Ten Commandments.
designations: International Criminal Court — Arrest warrant 2005 (12 counts crimes against humanity, 21 counts war crimes)

nodes.label: Chizuo Matsumoto
nodes.role: Founder and Guru of Aum Shinrikyo
nodes.summary: A partially-sighted yoga teacher, Asahara founded Aum Shinrikyo in 1984, blending elements of Tibetan Buddhism, Hindu Shaktism, Christian millenarianism, and Nostradamus prophecy into an apocalyptic doomsday cult. Aum recruited heavily among Japanese scientists and engineers, building a clandestine chemical and biological weapons programme.
designations: Japan — Convicted of 13 counts including murder; executed

nodes.label: Manuel Rubén Abimael Guzmán Reynoso
nodes.org: Partido Comunista del Perú – Sendero Luminoso (Shining Path)
nodes.role: Founder and 'Fourth Sword of Marxism' of Sendero Luminoso
nodes.summary: A philosophy professor at the National University of San Cristóbal de Huamanga in Ayacucho, Guzmán founded the Communist Party of Peru – Shining Path in 1970 as a Maoist splinter. He launched 'people's war' on 17 May 1980.
designations: US State Dept — Sendero Luminoso FTO
tags: Partido Comunista del Perú – Sendero Luminoso (Shining Path)

nodes.label: Ilich Ramírez Sánchez
nodes.role: 1970s–80s freelance terrorist operative; principal architect of the 1975 OPEC siege
nodes.summary: Born to a Marxist Venezuelan lawyer who named his three sons Ilich, Lenin, and Vladimir, Carlos studied at Patrice Lumumba University in Moscow and was recruited by Wadie Haddad's PFLP-EO.

nodes.label: Al-Qaeda
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Transnational salafi-jihadist organisation founded by Osama bin Laden in 1988.

nodes.label: Islamic State
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Salafi-jihadist organisation that declared a caliphate in 2014. Evolved from al-Qaeda in Iraq.

nodes.label: Taliban (Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Deobandi Islamist movement that has ruled Afghanistan 1996–2001 and 2021–present.

nodes.label: Haqqani Network
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Pashtun militant network based in Loya Paktia and North Waziristan; semi-autonomous Taliban faction.

nodes.label: Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Pakistan-based deobandi-jihadist umbrella organisation founded in 2007.

nodes.label: Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Punjab-based Ahl-e-Hadith militant organisation; anti-India operational focus.

nodes.label: Jaish-e-Mohammed (JeM)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Deobandi anti-India militant organisation founded by Masood Azhar in 2000.

nodes.label: Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Baloch ethno-nationalist secessionist armed group.

nodes.label: Balochistan Liberation Front (BLF)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Baloch ethno-nationalist secessionist armed group active in Makran.

nodes.label: Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Al-Qaeda's Yemen-based affiliate, formed in 2009.

nodes.label: Al-Qaeda in the Islamic Maghreb (AQIM)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Al-Qaeda's North Africa affiliate, evolved from Algeria's GSPC in 2007.

nodes.label: Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Al-Qaeda's Sahel coalition, founded in 2017 by Iyad ag Ghaly.

nodes.label: Islamic State in the Greater Sahara (ISGS)
nodes.org: Islamic State in the Greater Sahara (ISGS)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Islamic State's Sahel branch, founded in 2015 by al-Sahrawi.

nodes.label: Islamic State West Africa Province (ISWAP)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Islamic State's Lake Chad branch, split from Boko Haram in 2016.

nodes.label: Boko Haram
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Nigerian salafi-jihadist organisation founded c.2002.

nodes.label: Al-Shabaab
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Al-Qaeda's East African affiliate, controlling parts of southern Somalia.

nodes.label: Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Syrian Islamist movement that emerged from Jabhat al-Nusra; ruling party of transitional Syria since 2024.

nodes.label: Jemaah Islamiyah (JI)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Indonesian salafi-jihadist organisation co-founded by Abu Bakar Bashir.

nodes.label: Lord's Resistance Army (LRA)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Ugandan Christian-millenarian insurgency founded by Joseph Kony in 1987.

nodes.label: Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Tamil ethno-nationalist insurgency in Sri Lanka, defeated 2009.

nodes.label: Sendero Luminoso (Shining Path)
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Maoist insurgency in Peru, 1980–2000s.

nodes.label: Aum Shinrikyo
nodes.role: Militant network
nodes.summary: Japanese apocalyptic new religious movement responsible for the 1995 Tokyo subway sarin attack.

edges.label: Military Committee chief; de facto Emir
senior figurehead / member of Hittin Committee
head of military / external operations committee
Minister of Borders and Tribal Affairs
Acting Defence Minister; head of Military Commission
Operations chief; head of JI Mantiqi I
